rationale: Temporal hints are secondary; container routing is driven more by runtime and OS needs.
Container requests often involve OS/system-level operations and native dependencies.
Container workflows frequently execute CLI tools but are not necessarily skill-specific.
Document affinity is low unless the task also requires local binaries for conversion.
Containers may touch integrations, but protocol/server access is not the main signal.
Direct/simple phrasing usually lowers the need for a container runtime.
Time language matters less than artifact type for skill selection.
Most skills do not require raw OS access; they operate at workflow level.
Skill routing strongly correlates with specialized workflow/tool language.
Skills are highly aligned with document, slide, and spreadsheet deliverables.
Some skills call integrations, but integration is not their primary discriminator.
Very simple requests may bypass skills unless a deliverable format is explicit.
Cron routing is dominated by recurring temporal patterns and schedules.
System execution details matter, but only after schedule intent is detected.
Scheduler/tool hints support routing, but temporal recurrence is still primary.
Cron jobs rarely hinge on document-centric features.
Integrations can be scheduled, but they do not define cron intent on their own.
Simple phrasing usually implies one-shot behavior rather than scheduled persistence.
Scheduling language is mostly irrelevant to MCP routing.
Some MCP use cases touch systems, but protocol/server language is stronger.
MCP requests often include explicit tool-call semantics and capability discovery.
Document signals have low value unless the MCP server specifically handles documents.
MCP is fundamentally about external server-backed integrations and remote capabilities.
Plain direct wording rarely implies protocol-mediated tool use.
Temporal language usually points away from direct one-shot chat responses.
System-level signals generally indicate container or external execution instead.
Named tools reduce the likelihood of purely direct handling.
Some direct requests involve short document-like transformations, so affinity is moderate.
External-server language is typically absent in direct-chat requests.
Direct responses are strongly associated with plain-language, in-chat handling.
